Peace offerings showed respect for God as the source of all good things. They were divided between the altar, the priest, and the owner. These offerings were called peace offerings because they symbolized a feast shared by God and his people, signifying friendship. Peace offerings were offered as requests. If someone sought mercy, they would add a peace offering to their prayer for it. Christ is our Peace, our Peace offering; for through him alone we can receive an answer of peace to our prayers. Alternatively, the peace offering was given in thanks for a blessing received. We must offer God the sacrifice of praise continually, through Christ our Peace; and this will please the Lord more than an ox or bullock.

Here is a law that they should eat neither fat nor blood. The fat refers to the fat from the internal organs, such as suet. Blood was forbidden for the same reason: it belonged to God in every sacrifice. God would not allow the blood that made atonement to be treated as ordinary; nor will he allow us, even though we have the comfort of an atonement already made, to claim any part of the honor involved in making it. This taught the Jews to distinguish between common and sacred things; it kept them separate from idolaters. It would impress them more deeply with the belief of some important mystery in the shedding of blood and the burning of fat during their solemn sacrifices. Christ, as the Prince of peace, “made peace with the blood of his cross.” Through him the believer is reconciled to God; and having the peace of God in his heart, he is inclined to pursue peace with all people. May the Lord multiply grace, mercy, and peace, to all who desire to embody the Christian character.
